item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why not use the colbalt in a recoverable way? IE implant sticks filled with the stuff then take them back out when the area is clear?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good all round. I appreciate the dark, meta irony here – the anomalous aspects are just accessories to a mundane horror that does exist, and we created.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love this. Particularly the Cobalt-60 weapon containment proposal. Fits in very well with the theme of the article. 'In order to save the land from SCP-3968, it was necessary to destroy the land…' Same ends-justify-the-means pricks, different day. Sharp, clean bit of storytelling. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a really simple concept, a reproducing landmine, but I feel like it was executed very well. It flows well, with a simple plot and description.

All I can imagine when I read this is a guy in a EOD suit, defusing a landmine. An explosion goes off in the distance. A muffled cry of "GOD DAMNIT" is heard from the EOD suit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Glad you liked it. The theme of this was intended to be 'humans build some really shitty things' (which was incidentally why I included the lead-in descriptions of normal landmines, which I find scarier than a lot of monsters) - the letters and Vietnam refs were meant to maintain that connection.

I also thought the article might need a personal touch to a dry subject, but I can see why people would think the letters are out of place. Depending on how this pans out and what other feedback I get, I might consider replacing them with a more clinical document (like a manifest of items produced and buyers) or getting rid of them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Terrifying. I like the MCF connection in particular.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I felt the letters need more work. They don't seem to fit the tone imo to how what took place there. Seems a bit off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this a lot. It sort of ventures into the territory of "animal masquerading as a man-made object" which has been done a lot, but you don't dive into the biology or anything which I think was a smart move. As it is, it's a weird, fascinating thing.

Also really like the bit about irradiating areas as a form of containment and the shoutout to the ethics committee.

The only part I wasn't a huge fan of (and it wasn't bad, I just didn't love it) was the letters. I like pinning down an origin date for these things, but specifically talking about them being invented for the express purpose of ending the Vietnam war I thought was kind of weird, stylistically.

Still, easy +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I Like the concept, especially the descriptions of its tactics in dispersal and maximum causalities and how it displays some level of intelligence. Like others have said, a better hook further up the description would work better to draw the reader in, because all you are doing essentially is describing two different types of landmines without getting into its anomaly, which could put some readers off.

1+. Living, intelligent landmines that proliferate like plants with the set goal of killing and maiming is interesting. to me anyway.

makes me want to open a zoo for endangered landmines. poor things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, the description of it's 'behaviors' gives the reader some good hypothetical scenarios to think about in my opinion.

+1 from me :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is not a bad SCP at all. Good detail, reasonable containment procedures, everything. It's just a bit uninteresting. Maybe a more engaging hook?
